channel.title: Carat Capital — The Trade Paper of the Jewelry World
channel.description: Prices, intelligence and analysis from every desk of the stone trade: diamonds, gold and metals, gemstones, watches, auctions, retail and technology.

item.title: Down 3.4% in a war week: gold's oldest rule just broke
item.description: Spot gold closed Friday at $3,985.80 — its worst week in six — while airstrikes on Iran entered a sixth day. December rate-hike odds hit 73%, and the market decided a war can be a reason to sell bullion.

item.title: £105 million in, £7.8 million out: Audemars Piguet's British paradox
item.description: AP's UK sales jumped 29% in 2025 — nearly double their 2022 level — while operating profit slipped 3% to £7.8 million. The new Manchester AP House alone sold £7.4 million and still lost £738,000.

item.title: Serial 022 leaves the safe: the first Résonance ever sold heads to Geneva
item.description: One owner, 27 years, a serial number chosen to match his birth date. Phillips will offer F.P. Journe Chronomètre à Résonance no. 022/99R — almost certainly the first commercially delivered — on November 7–8, estimated at CHF 500,000 to 1 million.

item.title: Rolex, rejected: 38% fail one marketplace's inspection
item.description: Bezel turns away 38% of the Rolex watches submitted to its authenticators — against 13% for Omega, 6% for Tudor and 5% for Cartier. Rejected Rolexes account for 43% of all value refused; Patek Philippe another 22%.

item.title: Seventy dollars to Southampton: the jewelry trade pitches its summer tent
item.description: The Hamptons Jewelry Show returns July 23–26 — 36,000 square feet at the Southampton Fairgrounds, a $200 opening preview, $70 day passes, and two new pavilions aimed squarely at where American luxury money spends August.

item.title: Fifty years on Forty-Seventh: a dealer opens the books
item.description: Jeff Pancis walked into New York's Diamond District in 1976 and never left. His memoir 'Unveiling Rarities' covers five decades of stones, handshakes, a 1999 robbery that nearly ended the firm — and a trade that ran on memory.

item.title: Seven hundred members, 47 countries: color gets a new chief
item.description: Brandee Dallow — De Beers, Rio Tinto Diamonds, Grandview Klein, the Women's Jewelry Association — took over July 6 as CEO of the International Colored Gemstone Association, just as colored stones take a growing share of the counter.

item.title: Two hundred pieces, $250: Guess lets a machine draw its watch
item.description: The AI Venix — a 42mm tonneau in printed plastic, quartz inside, 200 pieces, web-only — is the first fashion watch to sell its AI-generated design as the feature. WatchPro's verdict: it could pass for a Richard Mille across a room.

item.title: Penny gets the nod: De Beers' old boss becomes its likeliest buyer
item.description: Anglo American names the Global Diamond Consortium, led by former De Beers chief executive Gareth Penny, preferred bidder for its 85% stake — carried at $4.9 billion after $3.5 billion of write-downs. Botswana, with 15% and matching rights, weighs its move.

item.title: $4,000, touched and defended: gold closes the week at 4,031
item.description: August futures opened Friday at $3,980.10 and spot sank toward the line before buyers arrived; by the New York evening gold stood at $4,031.02, up 1.2% on the day but 8.3% down on the month.

item.title: One carat wakes up; the price stays in bed
item.description: Rapaport's mid-July market comment finds one-carat demand picking up against falling supply — with prices unchanged. The premiums live in fancy shapes: long cushions fetch 20–25% over squares, and Belgium's half-year polished exports rose 18% to $3.7 billion.

item.title: 4.3 percent: China misses its floor and Geneva checks its books
item.description: Second-quarter GDP growth of 4.3% undershoots Beijing's 4.5–5% target, down from 5% in the first quarter. For Swiss watchmakers already nursing a 12.1% export drop to the mainland in 2025, the recovery just moved further out.

item.title: 22.6 percent: the shrinking price of Rolex's blessing
item.description: Rolex's certified pre-owned program commanded roughly 40% over the open secondary market at launch in December 2022. The median premium is now 22.6%, program sales hit $186 million in the second quarter, and 157 of 712 retailers carry the label.

item.title: Ninety percent sold: the trophy watch market clears the room
item.description: Sotheby's watch sales are up 64% in the first half; its latest auction drew 1,850 bidders from 60 countries, 90% of lots sold and 45% beat their high estimates. The million-dollar watch is becoming a category, not an event.

item.title: Zero, effective immediately: India's jewelry enters Britain duty-free
item.description: The India–UK trade pact took force July 15, and the first zero-duty consignments of gold, silver and diamond jewelry shipped within a day. Britain scraps duties on 96.8% of tariff lines; India's $13.44 billion export book eyes a bigger share.

item.title: £5 at the car boot; £15,000 under the loupe
item.description: A British bargain hunter paid £5 for what she took to be costume jewelry. At a valuation event in Oxfordshire it proved to be a 3.02-carat natural diamond in 18-karat gold — worth roughly £15,000, a three-thousand-fold return.

item.title: Woodley wears the Frida: HOWL's $98,000 collar goes campaigning
item.description: Miami jeweler HOWL — Handle Only With Love — debuts its Home Sweet Home campaign with Shailene Woodley in the Frida collar: 13 hand-carved Venetian glass pieces and 3.08 carats of old European-cut diamonds in 18-karat gold.

item.title: Ekati goes dark: Canada's first diamond mine finds no buyer
item.description: A British Columbia court has placed the Northwest Territories mine in receivership after an eight-week sale process drew no viable bid. Production stops in mid-August; about 340 workers remain from a 2024 payroll of 700 staff and 542 contractors.

item.title: Angola raises its hand: not a slice of De Beers, all of it
item.description: State miner Endiama has proposed buying Anglo American's entire 85 percent of De Beers, valued at $4.9 billion after $3.5 billion in write-downs. Botswana, holding 15 percent with the right to match, calls the prize a matter of sovereignty.

item.title: Minus 13, plus one: lab-grown finds its one-carat floor
item.description: Lab-grown wholesale prices fell 13 percent year on year in the second quarter, with two-carat rounds down 20 percent and squares down 21. One category rose: the one-carat round, up 1 percent, per Edahn Golan's Q2 price list.

item.title: Five days of airstrikes and gold still can't hold $4,050
item.description: Gold futures faded from a $4,068.90 open to $4,041 on Thursday as US strikes on Iranian sites entered a fifth day. Silver slipped to $57.07, down 16.8 percent in a month; platinum eased to $1,634.90.

item.title: Eighteen parts become one: Breitling puts the Chronomat on a diet
item.description: Breitling's remade Chronomat cuts case thickness from 15.1 to 13.77 millimeters, fuses an 18-component bezel into a single piece, and introduces the 78-hour B31 calibre, from £5,250, with the B01 chronograph at £7,850.

item.title: The Odyssey docks at the jewelry counter, pearls first
item.description: New York's Roseate has built a collection around Christopher Nolan's Odyssey: Tahitian and akoya pearls, engraved medallions from $275 to $325, and a Penelope-inspired Strength line, at Nordstrom from July 16.

item.title: Ten years from Kyiv to Wooster Street: Guzema opens in SoHo
item.description: Ukrainian fine jeweler Guzema opened its first US boutique on July 15 at 112 Wooster Street, an 1,100-square-foot space by architect Victoria Yakusha, marking the brand's tenth anniversary and a headquarters shift to New York.

item.title: $768.6 billion and a shrug: June retail grows 0.2 percent
item.description: US retail sales rose 0.2 percent in June to $768.6 billion, below expectations, as tax-refund tailwinds faded and tariff-era caution set in. For jewelers, the summer consumer is still spending, but no longer coasting.

item.title: Eleven lots, zero premium: Sotheby's quiet monthly experiment
item.description: The Gem Drop July closed in New York on July 16: eleven white-diamond jewels sold online with no buyer's premium, the sixth monthly installment of a format that strips out the fee auction houses have defended for decades.

item.title: Twenty percent: Richemont's quarter calls the luxury turn
item.description: Sales at the Cartier and Van Cleef & Arpels owner rose 20 percent at constant currency to 6.3 billion euros in the quarter to June 30, with the Americas up 27 percent and the watch division back to growth at 8 percent.

item.title: 28,000 diamonds, one lab: Vietnam's certificate scandal
item.description: The former director of P-Lab, the PNJ subsidiary controlling about 70 percent of Vietnam's diamond certification market, is among 22 people charged over a smuggling ring that moved 28,000-plus stones worth $10.65 million — allegedly re-papered with the lab's own certificates.

item.title: $8.9 billion half: the auction market buys its way back
item.description: Christie's posted $4.5 billion in first-half sales with public auctions up 71 percent; Sotheby's ran $4.4 billion including a record $826 million in private sales. Inside the numbers: younger buyers' average watch spend rose 60 percent to $129,000.

item.title: Four days of airstrikes, one flat tape: gold stalls at $4,045
item.description: Wednesday's session gave back a sliver of Tuesday's CPI rally — gold off 0.2 percent near $4,045 after an early run to $4,075, silver down 1.7 percent to $57.61 — even as US airstrikes on Iranian sites entered a fourth day.
